inline_review:
commit 1ede2d837f5cbf8e8d96fe4c6eab34aeaa071d56
Author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

mm/damon/core: validate damos_quota_goal->nid for node_memcg_{used,free}_bp

This commit adds validation for the node id in damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp()
to prevent out-of-bounds memory access. If an invalid node id is provided, it
now safely returns 0% for used memory and 100% for free memory.

> diff --git a/mm/damon/core.c b/mm/damon/core.c
> index 26bdeb658799..c45c62f8449d 100644
> --- a/mm/damon/core.c
> +++ b/mm/damon/core.c
> @@ -2273,6 +2273,13 @@ static unsigned long damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp(
>  	unsigned long used_pages, numerator;
>  	struct sysinfo i;
>  
> +	if (invalid_mem_node(goal->nid)) {
> +		if (goal->metric == DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP)
> +			return 0;
> +		else	/* DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP */
> +			return 10000;
> +	}
> +
>  	memcg = mem_cgroup_get_from_id(goal->memcg_id);
>  	if (!memcg) {
>  		if (goal->metric == DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP)

This isn't a bug introduced by this patch, but can the subtraction later in
this function underflow?

damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp() {
    ...
    else    /* DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP */
        numerator = i.totalram - used_pages;
    ...
}

Since used_pages is fetched locklessly and i.totalram is the global managed
pages count, could asynchronous rstat accounting lag or concurrent memory
hot-remove operations cause used_pages to transiently exceed i.totalram?

Also regarding the end of this function, is it possible for i.totalram to
evaluate to 0 here?

damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp() {
    ...
    return mult_frac(numerator, 10000, i.totalram);
}

If a node's memory is entirely reserved or concurrently offlined,
si_meminfo_node() might set i.totalram to 0. If that happens, does
mult_frac() execute an unchecked division by zero internally?

Finally, does the mult_frac() macro overflow unsigned long on 32-bit
architectures?

The macro evaluates to q * n_ + r * n_ / d_, where r is
numerator % i.totalram. On 32-bit systems, r is a 32-bit unsigned long.

For nodes with more than ~1.7GB of RAM (where i.totalram exceeds ~429,496
pages), could r * 10000 silently overflow the 32-bit boundary and return a
severely truncated value?

Target Commit:
commit 1ede2d837f5cbf8e8d96fe4c6eab34aeaa071d56
Author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
Date:   Sun Mar 29 04:39:00 2026 +0000

    mm/damon/core: validate damos_quota_goal->nid for node_memcg_{used,free}_bp
    
    Users can set damos_quota_goal->nid with arbitrary value for
    node_memcg_{used,free}_bp.  But DAMON core is using those for
    NODE-DATA() without a validation of the value.  This can result in out
    of bounds memory access.  The issue can actually triggered using DAMON
    user-space tool (damo), like below.
    
        $ sudo mkdir /sys/fs/cgroup/foo
        $ sudo ./damo start --damos_action stat --damos_quota_interval 1s \
                --damos_quota_goal node_memcg_used_bp 50% -1 /foo
        $ sudo dmseg
        [...]
        [  524.181426] Unable to handle kernel paging request at virtual address 0000000000002c00
    
    Fix this issue by adding the validation of the given node id.  If an
    invalid node id is given, it returns 0% for used memory ratio, and 100%
    for free memory ratio.
    
    Fixes: b74a120bcf50 ("mm/damon/core: implement DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP")
    Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org> # 6.19.x
    Signed-off-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

diff --git a/mm/damon/core.c b/mm/damon/core.c
index 26bdeb658799..c45c62f8449d 100644
--- a/mm/damon/core.c
+++ b/mm/damon/core.c
@@ -2273,6 +2273,13 @@ static unsigned long damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp(
 	unsigned long used_pages, numerator;
 	struct sysinfo i;
 
+	if (invalid_mem_node(goal->nid)) {
+		if (goal->metric == DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP)
+			return 0;
+		else	/* DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP */
+			return 10000;
+	}
+
 	memcg = mem_cgroup_get_from_id(goal->memcg_id);
 	if (!memcg) {
 		if (goal->metric == DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP)

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Analysis of DAMON Memory Node Validation**

Okay, let me break this down. This commit in `mm/damon/core.c` introduces a crucial validation check, `invalid_mem_node(goal->nid)`, within `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp()`. The purpose is clear: to prevent an out-of-bounds access, specifically when `goal->nid` holds an invalid node ID, potentially supplied from user-space (like a sneaky `-1`).

The existing code had a significant vulnerability: directly using `NODE_DATA(goal->nid)` could lead to accessing the `node_data` array outside of its bounds if `goal->nid` was invalid. The commit message highlights this specific issue, citing that an arbitrary `goal->nid` can be set (potentially via user-space interaction).

So, the new validation addresses this by checking for invalid nodes *before* the potentially disastrous `NODE_DATA` access. If `invalid_mem_node(goal->nid)` returns true, meaning the node is invalid, the code now returns 0 for `DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP` and 10000 (representing 100%) for `DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP`.

Now, is there anything fundamentally *wrong* with this approach? The intention is solid – it's a critical safety net against a kernel crash. The way it's implemented looks sound. Returning 100% free memory in the case of an invalid node makes logical sense; it's consistent with how the code already handled the case of a non-existent memcg.

Let's double-check the `invalid_mem_node()` function itself. Does it indeed validate bounds correctly? Yes, it includes the checks: `nid < 0 || nid >= MAX_NUMNODES || !node_state(nid, N_MEMORY)`. This covers the cases we expect.

My only momentary pause came when considering the implication of returning 0 for used memory and 100% for free memory when the node is invalid. Does this mean the quota will effectively *not* be applied or restricted? But after a moment's thought, that seems perfectly acceptable and indeed, is consistent with the goal of preventing crashes. It seems like a reasonable safe default, especially given the user-space inputs.

Are there any UAPI breakages? No, it looks like a clean internal fix.

Based on this analysis, I'm comfortable with this commit for Stage 1. No concerns.

Okay, so I'm diving into this commit and trying to understand its implications for DAMON quota management, especially around memory metrics. First, I noticed the commit message claims that invalid node IDs will result in 0% for used memory and 100% for free memory ratios, which seems like a sensible default to prevent incorrect quota decisions. The patch, with the conditional checks for `invalid_mem_node` and `!memcg`, supports this. Specifically, if a node ID is invalid, or the memory cgroup doesn't exist, it returns the expected safe values, returning `0` (used) and `10000` (free).

However, my attention then turned to the `!CONFIG_NUMA` case. Here, both `damos_get_node_mem_bp` and `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp` unconditionally return 0. If `goal->metric` is `DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP` or `DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEM_FREE_BP`, these functions also return 0. This is a problem! In systems without NUMA, free memory is *always* reported as 0%, which means the quota logic will think memory is 100% full, and aggressively throttle or reclaim resources, despite the actual situation.

But wait, `!CONFIG_NUMA` systems *do* have memory! They have Node 0. It's valid to access `NODE_DATA(0)`. The `DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP` is specific to per-node stats. Is it supported when NUMA is not configured? Although `NODE_DATA` still works, the unconditional returns of 0 for `!CONFIG_NUMA` are suspect. The intent is unclear, but the effect of reporting "0 free" is very dangerous. DAMON quota treats "0 free" as "maximum pressure," which triggers aggressive action.

I checked the commit history, and this behavior was introduced in commit `b74a120bcf50787e5b9a2c3dcff999f9836ce1db`. If these returns were intentionally compiled out for `!CONFIG_NUMA`, then the safe value would be `10000`, not `0` for `_FREE_BP`. The commit message is explicitly fixing "invalid node" logic, and in the context of `!CONFIG_NUMA` systems, that means returning a safe value for the node metrics.

So I've identified a *missing edge case*: the dummy functions for `!CONFIG_NUMA` always return 0, which incorrectly indicates 0% free memory for `DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEM_FREE_BP` and `DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP` metrics. The patch correctly ensures that under `CONFIG_NUMA`, the metrics return safe values for invalid nodes or missing memcgs, but the corresponding `#else` block is *not* updated and introduces a potential for severe memory pressure. This is a critical oversight.

The patch itself only modifies `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp` within the `CONFIG_NUMA` block, so it's a separate issue from the existing code. Commit `98fdce76fb7e` added `DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP`, which now needs a fix in the `#else` block, to handle the "invalid node" issue safely.

I also reviewed the code for `NODE_DATA(goal->nid)` accesses with respect to invalid nodes, and I confirmed that the patch correctly handles this case by validating the `nid` *before* accessing memory cgroup data, so this is safe. Similarly, I confirmed that `mem_cgroup_get_from_id` returns a NULL if the `memcg_id` is invalid, which is handled correctly.

Finally, I considered the possibility of `mult_frac(numerator, 10000, i.totalram)` causing a divide-by-zero if `i.totalram` is 0. While a valid node must have memory (`N_MEMORY` status), a node can have "present memory" but no managed pages because all memory is reserved (kernel text, memblock reserved). This results in `managed_pages` being 0, which becomes `i.totalram`, and so the division could result in a kernel panic! I need to ensure this is handled. This is another area for concern, independent of the `!CONFIG_NUMA` issue.

This is a significant problem because, if a user sets a goal using the `DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP` metric (which is, importantly, still *available* to be configured even without NUMA), the code will incorrectly interpret this as 0% free memory. This leads to extremely aggressive quota throttling, which directly contradicts the intended safe fallback behavior.  The `#else` block must be updated to correctly return `10000` (100%) for free memory metrics in the `!CONFIG_NUMA` case, just as it does in the NUMA case.

2.  **Arithmetic Underflow Risk**: When calculating free memory percentages, the code computes the numerator as `i.totalram - used_pages`. Both of these variables are unsigned integer types. However, there's a possibility that `used_pages` (which represents memory *used* by a memcg) could temporarily exceed `i.totalram` (the total managed memory for the node). This can happen because the memory usage is tracked in a per-CPU basis and, while it should be bounded by `i.totalram`, the code doesn't guarantee this condition and lacks lock synchronization. If this occurs, the subtraction will underflow, resulting in a large positive integer value for `numerator`.  Consequently, the percentage calculation will generate an astronomically high value. This must be corrected by clamping the value to `0` if `used_pages` exceeds `i.totalram`.  A safe and mathematically sound fix would be something along the lines of `numerator = i.totalram > used_pages ? i.totalram - used_pages : 0`.

3.  **Division by Zero Vulnerability**: The code concludes with `return mult_frac(numerator, 10000, i.totalram)`. The `mult_frac` macro, which is standard in the kernel, performs a division by its third argument, which is `i.totalram`. It uses integer arithmetic designed to avoid overflow. A valid NUMA node can have zero managed pages, for example, if all pages are reserved for other kernel functions, or while the memory is being offlined. If a user sets a quota goal on such a node, the resulting division by zero will cause an immediate kernel panic.  A check, such as `if (i.totalram == 0) return (metric == DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP ? 0 : 10000);` is required before the division to avoid the crash. Okay, so I'm looking at this code where `sysfs_goal->nid` is set from userspace and copied to `goal->nid`. It seems straightforward enough. If `goal->nid` is invalid, the code returns either 0 or 10000, which feels like a placeholder or an error indicator. But I'm starting to see some red flags.

Specifically, I'm concerned about this snippet:

```c
	if (invalid_mem_node(goal->nid)) {
		if (goal->metric == DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP)
			return 0;
		else	/* DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP */
			return 10000;
	}
```

My initial thought was that returning 10000 in the `DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP` case seemed a bit arbitrary. However, the bigger issue is in the `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp` function:

```c
	si_meminfo_node(&i, goal->nid);
	if (goal->metric == DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP)
		numerator = used_pages;
	else	/* DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP */
		numerator = i.totalram - used_pages;
	return mult_frac(numerator, 10000, i.totalram);
```

The thing is, `i.totalram` *could* be zero, right? What happens if `i.totalram` is zero and the code executes `mult_frac(numerator, 10000, 0)`? I'm pretty sure that's going to cause a kernel panic, right? Let's verify with the definition of `mult_frac`:

```c
/* Calculate "x * n / d" without unnecessary overflow or loss of precision. */
#define mult_frac(x, n, d)	\
({				\
	typeof(x) x_ = (x);	\
	typeof(n) n_ = (n);	\
	typeof(d) d_ = (d);	\
				\
	typeof(x_) q = x_ / d_;	\
	typeof(x_) r = x_ % d_;	\
	q * n_ + r * n_ / d_;	\
})
```

Yep, as expected. If `d_` (which is `i.totalram`) is zero, both the division and the modulo operations become division by zero, which is a big, flashing error signal in kernel land. This would cause a definite crash.

The `invalid_mem_node()` check *should* prevent this but let me confirm.

```c
static bool invalid_mem_node(int nid)
{
	return nid < 0 || nid >= MAX_NUMNODES || !node_state(nid, N_MEMORY);
}
```

I'm thinking, does `node_state(nid, N_MEMORY)` guarantee that `i.totalram` is not zero? I know that `node_state()` indicates if the node has memory but does that *always* mean `i.totalram` will be non-zero after `si_meminfo_node()` is called? I don't think so. The fact that the node exists doesn't mean that the memory is actually allocated or that `i.totalram` is filled properly with some non-zero value. I'm going to need to investigate what `si_meminfo_node()` does to confirm.

    "content": "{"content":"# SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0-only\n\nmenu \"Data Access Monitoring\"\n\nconfig DAMON\n\tbool \"DAMON: Data Access Monitoring Framework\"\n\thelp\n\t  This builds a framework that allows kernel subsystems to monitor\n\t  access frequency of each memory region. The information can be useful\n\t  for performance-centric DRAM level memory management.\n\n\t  See https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/latest/mm/damon/index.html for\n\t  more information.\n\nconfig DAMON_DEBUG_SANITY\n\tbool \"Check sanity of DAMON code\"\n\tdepends on DAMON\n\thelp\n\t  This enables additional DAMON debugging-purpose sanity checks in\n\t  DAMON code. Okay, I'm looking at this kernel code and something's not quite right. I noticed these function declarations in `include/linux/mm.h`:

```c
extern long si_mem_available(void);
extern void si_meminfo(struct sysinfo * val);
extern void si_meminfo_node(struct sysinfo *val, int nid);
```

Specifically, I'm focusing on `si_meminfo_node`. Now, the declaration itself *isn't* guarded by a `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA`, which is unusual. But then, I checked the definition in `mm/show_mem.c`, and there it *is*:

```c
#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA
void si_meminfo_node(struct sysinfo *val, int nid)
{ ... }
#endif
```

My brain just went *ping*. If `CONFIG_NUMA` is disabled, then `si_meminfo_node` won't be defined! That’s an immediate red flag. The potential for a linking error just clicked. If `CONFIG_DAMON` is enabled, but `CONFIG_NUMA` isn't, and some code within `mm/damon/core.c` calls `si_meminfo_node`, the linker will complain about an undefined symbol. This is textbook build failure territory.

Now I need to check something *crucial*. Does `mm/damon/core.c` conditionally include the call to `si_meminfo_node` using `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA`? I need to go back and carefully scrutinize the usage in that file. That's the only way to determine if this is a real problem or a false alarm.

Okay, first things first, good to see that the `invalid_mem_node`, `damos_get_node_mem_bp`, and `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp` functions are all guarded by `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA`. No linking errors to worry about there. My initial concern was a divide-by-zero, let's trace this back, the root cause is in `mult_frac(numerator, 10000, i.totalram)`. Let's step through the code:

When calling `si_meminfo_node`, it seems that `managed_pages` is accumulated. However, could `managed_pages` ever be zero for a node that actually *has* memory? Turns out, yes. A node can be `N_MEMORY` (meaning it's physically present) but the buddy allocator might not be managing any of its memory, because the memory is reserved. In that scenario, `zone_managed_pages()` will sum to zero. And if `managed_pages` is `0`, then `val->totalram` will be `0`!

So, `i.totalram` can definitely be zero. If that happens in `mult_frac(numerator, 10000, i.totalram)`, we *will* get a divide-by-zero kernel panic. Does the current patch address this? No, it only checks `invalid_mem_node(goal->nid)`.

Now, let's step back a bit and consider how the patch *should* handle an invalid node ID:

The original code in `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp` used to call `NODE_DATA(goal->nid)`. If `goal->nid` was out of bounds, like `-1`, it would lead to an out-of-bounds array access on the `node_data` array, which would crash with `Unable to handle kernel paging request`. This is exactly the problem that the patch aims to fix.

The patch *does* guard against an invalid node ID using `invalid_mem_node` and returns `0` or `10000`. So, if the node is *invalid*, the patch *correctly* avoids the divide by zero. Specifically, if the node is invalid, `USED_BP` returns 0%, and `FREE_BP` returns 100%.

However, what if the node *is* valid, but `i.totalram` happens to be zero?

Now, looking at `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp` again, the code reads the following:
```c
	mem_cgroup_flush_stats(memcg);
	lruvec = mem_cgroup_lruvec(memcg, NODE_DATA(goal->nid));
	used_pages = lruvec_page_state(lruvec, NR_ACTIVE_ANON);
```
If `i.totalram` is zero, we should definitely check for `!i.totalram` before attempting any division. It seems to me, if `i.totalram` is zero, we should return `0` for `USED_BP` and `10000` for `FREE_BP`, just like the invalid node case.

A remaining question here is about `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp` code. This is a bit odd! The function's name is `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp` and yet it handles *both* `USED_BP` and `FREE_BP`.

    "thought": "**Analysis of Divide-by-Zero and Node Validity in Memory Quota Code**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial concern was the potential for a divide-by-zero error within the `damos_get_in_active_mem_bp` function. The core issue is the `mult_frac` function's second argument (`total`) possibly being zero when calculating the ratio of active or inactive memory. While I acknowledged that `total` *could* be zero, I quickly realized that this wasn't the primary flaw. The real vulnerability lies in the use of `i.totalram` as a divisor in `damos_get_node_mem_bp` and, by extension, in the modified `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp`.

The crucial question becomes: When might `i.totalram` be zero even when the node is seemingly valid (i.e., it has memory). My understanding is that `i.totalram` is populated from `managed_pages`. A node can *have* memory (as indicated by the `N_MEMORY` flag being set and validated by `invalid_mem_node`), but it can also have *zero* managed pages. This can happen if all memory on a node is reserved for kernel use (e.g., kernel text, memblock reserved regions) and not available for the buddy allocator to manage. In this scenario, `i.totalram` is zero, leading to the potential for a division by zero error in `mult_frac`.

The patch itself addresses an existing issue by adding a check for `invalid_mem_node` to `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp`. This check essentially replicates what was already present in `damos_get_node_mem_bp`. The check's purpose is to return either 0 or 10000 (representing percentages) if the node ID is invalid. This is intended behavior to handle invalid nodes that were being passed in by a DAMON core without validation.

However, the question that I have is whether the behavior for `NUMA_NO_NODE` is correct. The user *can* set `goal->nid` to `-1` through the sysfs interface. If they do, this will cause the `invalid_mem_node` function to return true, and the function will return either 0 or 10000. Is this expected behavior? Is it really the intent of the quota to say "zero percent used" or "100 percent free" when the user specifies `-1`? The documentation says the behavior is for *invalid* node IDs, but is `-1` equivalent to *invalid* or is it more like saying "all nodes"? Shouldn't it sum up over all nodes instead of returning 0? I should verify this user intent.

The code itself appears sound in the sense of avoiding obvious memory leaks with `mem_cgroup_put` in `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp`. However, I need to ensure that `NODE_DATA(goal->nid)` is always valid when `invalid_mem_node` returns `false`, and `mem_cgroup_lruvec` isn't returning NULL. The use of `node_state(nid, N_MEMORY)` ensures that `NODE_DATA(nid)` is valid for online nodes with memory.

However, it is structurally possible for a NUMA node to have physical memory but 0 managed pages (e.g., if all of its memory is entirely reserved). In such a scenario, `i.totalram` will be exactly 0. The `mult_frac` macro expands to perform division and modulo operations by the denominator (`q = x_ / d_; r = x_ % d_;`). Passing 0 as the denominator will trigger a divide-by-zero kernel panic. A zero-check on `i.totalram` should be added before calling `mult_frac()`, treating `i.totalram == 0` appropriately (e.g. 0% used and 100% free)."

Using `pgdat->node_id` in that context could lead to a hard crash, and that's the kind of thing that keeps me up at night.  But wait... wasn't there some check to ensure the validity of `NODE_DATA(nid)`?  Oh, right, `invalid_mem_node` is there to ensure `NODE_DATA(nid)` is valid. So if it gets past this check, `pgdat` must be valid, correct?

Now, a more subtle question: is `memcg->nodeinfo` allocated for *all* possible NUMA nodes in the system, or just the currently online nodes? This is a crucial detail for memory allocation and potential resource contention. I should really check how `memcg->nodeinfo` is actually allocated to be certain.     "thought": "**Critical Bug: Division by Zero in DAMOS Quota Calculation**

Okay, let's break this down. First, the code seems generally safe in terms of memory access within the `memcg` structure. We are properly handling the `nodeinfo` array and related memory access.  No immediate RCU violations are apparent. No new locks are introduced, so lock ordering seems fine. However, I need to focus on potential user input issues and calculations.

My main concern revolves around the potential for division by zero in the `mult_frac` function.  Specifically:

```c
si_meminfo_node(&i, goal->nid);
if (goal->metric == DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP)
    numerator = used_pages;
else    /* DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP */
    numerator = i.totalram - used_pages;
return mult_frac(numerator, 10000, i.totalram);
```

The problem is `i.totalram`.  If this value is zero, we'll get a division by zero error in `mult_frac`, which triggers a kernel panic. I've confirmed that `i.totalram` is calculated based on buddy allocator managed pages, which means we can have situations where `totalram` is zero.

The code path through `damos_get_node_mem_bp` has the same issue.

The function `invalid_mem_node()` makes sure that the node id is valid, but *does not* guarantee that `totalram` is non-zero!

This is where it gets interesting:

1.  **Reserved Memory/Early Boot:**  If a node's memory is reserved during boot using `memblock_reserve`, it won't be managed by the buddy allocator.  Therefore `i.totalram` will be zero.
2.  **Memory Offline/Zone Device:** A node can have `N_MEMORY` set (meaning it has memory), but `i.totalram` can be 0. We've considered memory offlining. If a node is offlined, its managed pages become zero. If all the memory of a node is offlined but not removed (e.g. not hot-removed), `N_MEMORY` still stays set, because the sections remain. In this case, `node_spanned_pages` remains greater than zero and `totalram` becomes zero.

A user could intentionally trigger this vulnerability by specifying a node ID where `i.totalram` is zero. Furthermore, `ZONE_DEVICE` memory (e.g., persistent memory or device memory) doesn't contribute to managed pages, and can cause this.

Therefore, this is a very serious bug that needs immediate attention. The code needs to check for `i.totalram == 0` and handle this gracefully, for example, by returning an error or a reasonable default value, or by not doing the calculation if `i.totalram` is zero. A simple division-by-zero check is all that is required.

    "thought": "**DAMON Quota Goal Analysis**

Okay, let's break this down. It seems we're dealing with a DAMON quota system, specifically how quota goals are set via sysfs and how those goals are *used* internally.  My first thought is the initial translation from the sysfs representation (`damos_sysfs_quota_goal`) to the internal structure (`damos_quota_goal`) is safe due to the `damos_new_quota_goal` function. This process happens during the `damon_sysfs_set_schemes()` call. And the process of setting the goal is safe because it is copied internally and no concurrent access is possible.

Now, the sysfs files are essentially configuration points. You set them, and then a separate action (like writing "commit" to a state file) triggers the configuration's application. The key point is that the internal `damos_quota_goal` structure, from which values are read (e.g., in `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp()`), is not directly accessible via sysfs *after* its creation. This prevents potential Time-of-Check-Time-of-Use (TOCTOU) vulnerabilities that might otherwise allow concurrent modifications, which is good.

Okay, so the initial concern about `goal->nid` not being properly validated has been addressed in the code already, and negative values are handled. But here's where it gets interesting: the commit message mentions possible integer issues related to the `goal->nid` value. So, I need to look into other integer-related concerns. Specifically, can `totalram` be zero?

The `si_meminfo_node` function calculates `totalram`. It iterates through memory zones and sums `managed_pages`. If all managed pages are zero, `totalram` becomes zero. Now, can this happen? The Red Team guidance highlights that it is essential to look at possible flaws and not assume the system perfectly handles every case. It might be possible that a memory node is online and has memory state `N_MEMORY`, but `totalram` is zero. A large number of hugepages at boot time might do it. If so, a division-by-zero could occur: `mult_frac(numerator, 10000, i.totalram)`. This is a crash vector, and I have to explore the possibility.

The code does correctly handle the case of an invalid memory node, specifically with `NUMA_NO_NODE` which is gracefully handled because `invalid_mem_node()` returns true if `nid < 0` or if the node is not `N_MEMORY`. This is good. It checks `!node_state(nid, N_MEMORY)`.
And it also checks the `memcg` which is also good.

However, the bigger concern is the potential underflow in the calculation of `numerator`. The formula is:

*   If `goal->metric == DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP`:  `numerator = used_pages;`
*   Else (`DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP`): `numerator = i.totalram - used_pages;`

Now, if `used_pages > i.totalram`, then there is an integer underflow. `used_pages` is derived from per-cpu counters, so drift in these counters could momentarily make `used_pages` larger than `i.totalram`.  If the underflow happens, this would result in a huge positive number. But it becomes worse; the `mult_frac` is:

`#define mult_frac(x, n, d) ...`

`mult_frac` in this case will have a large positive `numerator`, and might cause overflow, which might result in an incorrect quota goal and thus either too aggressive or not enough reclamation, and this is bad.

So the concern is: *is* the assumption that `used_pages <= i.totalram` guaranteed? Given the potential for per-cpu counter drift, it's not a safe assumption. This underflow can lead to undefined or garbage behavior when used for quota settings. An underflow bug could cause this, and needs to be addressed. It's likely an underflow bug could cause problems.  I should bound the `numerator` to prevent this!

    "thought": "**Analysis of `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp` Function**

Okay, here's my breakdown of the code. My initial thought process revolves around potential vulnerabilities and error conditions. The core logic calculates memory usage and free space for a cgroup on a specific node.

First, I see the potential for integer overflow in the `mult_frac` macro, particularly when calculating the quota. If `used_pages` is close to the maximum value of `unsigned long` and we're subtracting it from `i.totalram`, the numerator will be a very large number. Multiplying this large numerator by 10000 and dividing by `i.totalram` could lead to overflow. This will, however, not lead to a crash; it would merely return a garbage quota.

Now, I was initially worried about a possible division by zero error if `i.totalram` is zero. Upon closer inspection, I see that this is possible. `i.totalram` is assigned the value of `managed_pages` which can, in theory, be zero if the node has been configured in such a way. I can confirm this by looking into the code for `si_meminfo_node` where `managed_pages` is computed, and also checking how memory nodes are added to a system.  The attacker can trigger this condition by specifying a node with no available memory, leading to a kernel panic. I've realized this is not unique to the patched code; `damos_get_node_mem_bp` has the exact same vulnerability.

Regarding the calculation of `used_pages`, I was concerned that the sum of `NR_ACTIVE_ANON`, `NR_INACTIVE_ANON`, `NR_ACTIVE_FILE`, and `NR_INACTIVE_FILE` *could* be larger than `i.totalram` for a number of reasons. For example, if there were memory accounting bugs or stats drift. After verifying, it should *not* happen because `lruvec_page_state` clamps to 0 in case of underflow. The only way it's larger is if `i.totalram` is 0. This is also covered by the division by zero condition mentioned above.

I also checked the `memcg` management: I looked at how the `memcg` is acquired using `mem_cgroup_get_from_id` and then subsequently released with `mem_cgroup_put` to ensure there aren't any use-after-free conditions. The code correctly acquires and releases the `memcg` reference, making this safe.

I was also concerned about the possible manipulation of `goal->memcg_id` or `goal->metric`. The `memcg_id` is passed through `cgroup_get_from_id`, which is safe because it performs a safe lookup. An attacker can't inject an arbitrary ID. The metric, however, is a potential issue. If the metric is not `DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP` or `DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP`, it is treated as a `FREE_BP`. This isn't a *vulnerability* but is a deviation from the expected behavior.

mm/damon/core: validate damos_quota_goal->nid for node_memcg_{used,free}_bp

This commit adds validation for the node id in damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp()
to prevent out-of-bounds memory access. If an invalid node id is provided, it
now safely returns 0% for used memory and 100% for free memory.

> diff --git a/mm/damon/core.c b/mm/damon/core.c
> index 26bdeb658799..c45c62f8449d 100644
> --- a/mm/damon/core.c
> +++ b/mm/damon/core.c
> @@ -2273,6 +2273,13 @@ static unsigned long damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp(
>  	unsigned long used_pages, numerator;
>  	struct sysinfo i;
>  
> +	if (invalid_mem_node(goal->nid)) {
> +		if (goal->metric == DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP)
> +			return 0;
> +		else	/* DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP */
> +			return 10000;
> +	}
> +
>  	memcg = mem_cgroup_get_from_id(goal->memcg_id);
>  	if (!memcg) {
>  		if (goal->metric == DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP)

This isn't a bug introduced by this patch, but can the subtraction later in
this function underflow?

damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp() {
    ...
    else    /* DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP */
        numerator = i.totalram - used_pages;
    ...
}

Since used_pages is fetched locklessly and i.totalram is the global managed
pages count, could asynchronous rstat accounting lag or concurrent memory
hot-remove operations cause used_pages to transiently exceed i.totalram?

Also regarding the end of this function, is it possible for i.totalram to
evaluate to 0 here?

damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp() {
    ...
    return mult_frac(numerator, 10000, i.totalram);
}

If a node's memory is entirely reserved or concurrently offlined,
si_meminfo_node() might set i.totalram to 0. If that happens, does
mult_frac() execute an unchecked division by zero internally?

Finally, does the mult_frac() macro overflow unsigned long on 32-bit
architectures?

The macro evaluates to q * n_ + r * n_ / d_, where r is
numerator % i.totalram. On 32-bit systems, r is a 32-bit unsigned long.

For nodes with more than ~1.7GB of RAM (where i.totalram exceeds ~429,496
pages), could r * 10000 silently overflow the 32-bit boundary and return a
severely truncated value?

",
